column1: Apex Retail — Retention KPI Navigation Map
KPIs by navigation area, with refresh cadence, type (B / LE / LA / G), plain detail, an explanation, why it matters to the business case, the deck's target where stated, and the formula.

column7: What the KPI is (explanation)
column8: Why it matters (business case)
column10: Formula

column7: Share of customers who leave over a year, shown against the program goal.
column8: The headline problem — 28% is 3x the retail norm and the whole program exists to cut it.
column10: (customers lost in period ÷ customers at period start) × 100

column7: Share of enrolled loyalty members who actually redeem a reward, vs goal.
column8: The scorecard on the $2.1M platform — 0% redemption meant it changed no behaviour.
column10: (members redeeming ≥1 reward in 90 days ÷ enrolled members) × 100

column7: Live dollar estimate of annual revenue tied to customers currently at high risk.
column8: Turns risk into money leadership feels; anchors urgency against the $14.2M lost.
column10: Σ (annual value of each customer currently scored High-risk)

column7: Average time from a churn signal firing to a retention campaign going live.
column8: The timing fix — the old 4–7 day lag meant the window shut before anyone acted.
column10: mean( timestamp(campaign live) − timestamp(signal detected) )

column7: Share of offers matched to a customer's actual signals rather than a blanket coupon.
column8: The generic 10% voucher is why offers were ignored; relevance is what makes people stay.
column10: (offers matched to customer's signal profile ÷ total offers sent) × 100

column6: High / Med / Low vs prior period
column7: Count of customers in High / Medium / Low risk bands, vs the prior period.
column8: The at-a-glance pulse — tells the team where the book is heading right now.
column10: count(customers) grouped by band (High 71–100, Med 41–70, Low 0–40); trend = this period − prior

column7: Real-time stream of customers newly crossing the composite-score threshold.
column8: Makes detection visible and immediate — the opposite of finding out days later.
column10: count(customers whose composite score crossed threshold in last 24h), streamed as events

column7: Number of briefs awaiting human sign-off and the oldest wait time.
column8: Guards the human-in-the-loop gate from becoming the new bottleneck.
column10: count(briefs where status = pending); oldest wait = max(now − brief created time)

column7: Live status of all six source connectors (green / amber / red).
column8: If a source silently drops, the score is computed on partial data and quietly misleads.
column10: per connector: green if last-sync < 15 min, amber < 60 min, red ≥ 60 min or error

column7: A single 0–100 risk score built from all six systems' signals.
column8: The core invention — one number no single system could produce; it drives the ranking.
column10: Σ (signal weight × normalised signal value 0–1) × 100  — see 'Composite Score Model'

column7: Combined lifetime value of every customer currently in the queue.
column8: The 'value' half of risk x value — focuses effort on customers worth saving.
column10: Σ (lifetime value of each customer in the queue)

column7: Forecast of how many customers will become high-risk in the next 30 days.
column8: Lets the team staff for what's coming, not just work the current backlog.
column10: model forecast of count(customers expected to cross High threshold in next 30 days)

column7: Split of the queue across High / Medium / Low score bands.
column8: Shows the shape of demand so triage and offers can be prioritised.
column10: count(queue customers) grouped by score band

column7: Share of contacted at-risk customers still retained 90 days later.
column8: The bottom-line proof the intervention actually works, not just fires fast.
column10: (at-risk customers retained 90 days after contact ÷ at-risk customers contacted) × 100

column6: Keeps the queue from backing up
column7: Customers actioned today vs customers added to the queue today.
column8: Early warning that the queue is silently growing faster than it's worked.
column10: count(customers actioned today) ÷ count(added to queue today)

column7: This customer's 0–100 score and its risk band.
column8: Anchors the single-customer story in one comparable, cited number.
column10: the weighted composite formula, computed for this customer

column7: Change in this customer's web sessions vs the prior period.
column8: The earliest signal — browsing collapse precedes churn by 2–3 weeks.
column10: (sessions last 30d − sessions prior 30d) ÷ sessions prior 30d × 100

column7: How long since this customer last redeemed a loyalty reward.
column8: Zero redemption was the single strongest churn signal in the source data.
column10: days since last redemption, or count(redemptions in window)

column7: Change in this customer's average order value vs their baseline.
column8: Falling spend and discount dependency signal margin erosion before churn.
column10: (recent avg order value − baseline AOV) ÷ baseline AOV × 100

column7: Share of emails this customer opens.
column8: Engagement collapse shows here first — a mentally-churned customer stops opening.
column10: (emails opened ÷ emails delivered) × 100

column7: Days this customer's oldest unresolved support ticket has been open.
column8: An unresolved ticket before churn is a proven precursor invisible to the CRM.
column10: now − oldest unresolved ticket created date

column7: Flag when the CRM rates a customer healthy but the composite says high-risk.
column8: This is the whole thesis — the CRM scored a churner 72/100 'healthy'.
column10: flag when CRM health score ≥ 70 AND composite score ≥ 71

column7: The offer the agent recommends for this customer, with a confidence level.
column8: Turns the score into an action a human can approve in one click.
column10: rule/model: score band × dominant signal × customer tier → offer; confidence = model probability

column7: Each signal's contribution to this customer's total score.
column8: Makes the score explainable, so the human trusts and can challenge it.
column10: per signal: (weight × normalised value), shown as its share of the total score

column7: Whether every signal in the brief names the system it came from.
column8: Traceability is what makes the brief defensible and auditable.
column10: citation coverage = (signals with a named source ÷ signals used) × 100  — target 100%

column7: Expected value of the offer: save probability x LTV minus offer cost.
column8: Ensures the recommended action is worth more than it costs.
column10: expected value = P(save | offer) × customer LTV − offer cost

column7: Time from signal detected to campaign live.
column8: Acting within the window is what converts early warning into a save.
column10: timestamp(campaign live) − timestamp(signal detected)

column7: Share of sent offers matched to the customer's signal profile.
column8: Relevant offers get opened and used; generic ones get ignored.
column10: (offers matched to signal profile ÷ total offers sent) × 100

column7: Open, click and redeem rates for retention campaigns.
column8: Confirms the outreach actually landed, not just that it was sent.
column10: open / click / redeem rate — each (action count ÷ delivered) × 100

column7: Share of contacted at-risk customers still retained 90 days later.
column8: The bottom-line proof the intervention actually works, not just fires fast.
column10: (contacted at-risk retained 90d ÷ contacted at-risk) × 100

column7: Unsubscribes and opt-outs following retention contact.
column8: Over-contacting at-risk customers can cause the very churn you're preventing.
column10: (unsubscribes + opt-outs after retention contact ÷ retention contacts) × 100

column7: Share of customers lost over a year.
column8: The core outcome the business case is judged on.
column10: (customers lost in year ÷ customers at year start) × 100

column7: Annual revenue kept by reducing churn.
column8: Translates the churn win into the dollars that justify the $2.1M spend.
column10: (baseline churn % − current churn %) × avg annual revenue per customer × customer base

column7: Share of members redeeming a reward within 90 days.
column8: Proof the loyalty platform is finally changing behaviour.
column10: (members redeeming ≥1 reward in 90 days ÷ enrolled members) × 100

column7: Share of contacted at-risk customers still retained 90 days later.
column8: The bottom-line proof the intervention actually works, not just fires fast.
column10: (contacted at-risk retained 90d ÷ contacted at-risk) × 100

column7: Average post-interaction satisfaction score for loyalty.
column8: A softer outcome that shows retention isn't bought at the cost of experience.
column10: mean(post-interaction survey scores)

column7: Days of warning between first signal and confirmed churn.
column8: The differentiator — 14 days of runway is what makes cross-system worth it.
column10: date(churn confirmed) − date(first signal crossed threshold)

column7: Model projection of churn rate at quarter end.
column8: Tells leadership whether the program is on track before the quarter closes.
column10: model projection of churn rate at period end

column7: Count of customers churned over the last 90 days.
column8: The lagging count that confirms the rate is genuinely improving.
column10: count(customers with no purchase in 180 days) over the last 90-day window

column7: Average order-value decline across at-risk customers.
column8: Confirms whether spend erosion (a churn precursor) is reversing.
column10: (current avg order value − baseline AOV) ÷ baseline AOV × 100

column7: Human hours to produce a retention brief per segment.
column8: The efficiency lever — 4–8 hrs to under 5 min frees the team to act.
column10: total analyst hours ÷ segment runs

column7: Retention lift of a helped group vs an untreated control group.
column8: The honest proof that offers caused the saves, not luck — makes '$7M' real.
column10: retention rate(treated group) − retention rate(control holdout)

column7: Discount and reward cost divided by customers saved.
column8: Stops the team 'winning' churn with discounts that cost more than they save.
column10: (Σ discount + reward cost of retention offers) ÷ customers saved

column7: Uptime, last-sync and staleness for each of the six connectors.
column8: Integration health is the foundation — bad data silently corrupts every score.
column10: uptime % = (successful syncs ÷ attempted syncs) × 100; staleness = now − last successful sync

column6: Of those flagged High, how many churned
column7: Of customers flagged high-risk, the share that actually churned.
column8: Answers 'does the score work?' — precision stops false alarms.
column10: true positives ÷ (true positives + false positives)

column6: Of churners, how many were flagged
column7: Of customers who churned, the share the model flagged.
column8: The other half of accuracy — recall stops missed churners.
column10: true positives ÷ (true positives + false negatives)

column7: Share of high-risk flags that did not churn.
column8: Too many false alarms train the whole base to expect discounts.
column10: (flagged High who did NOT churn ÷ total flagged High) × 100

column7: How often humans override the agent, with logged reasons.
column8: A trust signal and a free feedback loop for improving the model.
column10: (briefs overridden ÷ briefs reviewed) × 100

column7: Share of actions logged with full source citations.
column8: Compliance and defensibility — the old process had no audit trail at all.
column10: (actions with full source citation ÷ total actions) × 100

column7: Which scoring version is live and whether its output has drifted.
column8: Governance — ensures the score stays valid as customer behaviour shifts.
column10: current score distribution vs baseline (e.g. population stability index)
